hypothesis: The strength of a metal lattice's chemical template effect—quantified by the degree of electron localization at interstitial quasi-atom sites—will systematically correlate with the optimal hydrogen cage geometry and hydrogen-to-metal ratio in the resulting superhydride, such that metal lattices exhibiting anisotropic quasi-atom distributions will preferentially stabilize non-integer stoichiometries with mixed cage motifs (e.g., coexisting H24 and H32 cages) rather than uniform clathrate frameworks, and this correlation can be learned and predicted by a graph neural network trained on electron localization function topologies, enabling a priori cage-type classification before any structural search is performed.
